Hey there, in the contract slash waiver that we have, I recommend you use it. It's an awesome contract. It's also a great sales contract. It has all these things in there. The way that you use it is it's awesome to give them beforehand to have them circle the times that they can attend the sessions prior to bringing up the price and ask them to circle all the sessions that they think they could attend. So not the ones that they will attend, but the ones that they think they could attend, which then gives you even more reason that you can say, hey, listen, you can make plenty of these sessions, right? And so it overcomes the scheduling. I'm not sure if I can make it, blah, blah, blah. It overcomes that one. The next one is underneath of that, it has all the controllable health risks that you're going to have them go through and estimate how much money they're spending each week slash month on these controllable unhealthy habits. It's great because when you have money objections, you can overcome them because almost everyone spends a couple hundred bucks a month on unhealthy things like everyone does. So all you need to do is just not make them feel, just make it feel natural for them to fill that out so you're finding more information out about them, but then you'll use that at the end to circle back. And I mean, we do this stuff because we want to help people and we want to get this stuff out of their own way, get them out of their own way so they can make the right decision for their own health. The last thing you need to do is make sure that you edit the contract so that if it says get fit with Ty, so you can just do control F, get fit with Ty, and it's put in your gym name or whatever, it's going to appear on their credit card so that that's what's in the contract so it sounds like it makes sense. But that's the contract I recommend you use. It's a great sales piece as well as a formidable contract. So rock and roll and execute.
